title: Multiple Categories

questions.question: What chart type is useful for visualizing the differences in representation of ethnicities on jury panels?
questions.answer: A bar chart is useful for visualizing the differences in representation of ethnicities on jury panels.

questions.question: What does sampling with replacement mean?
questions.answer: Sampling with replacement means each individual is returned to the population after being chosen, allowing them to be selected more than once.

questions.question: How does the distribution of a random sample compare to that of the population?
questions.answer: The distribution of a random sample closely matches the distribution of the eligible population when the sample size is appropriately chosen, unlike the distribution of the panels.

questions.question: How can you assess whether a random sample represents a general pattern?
questions.answer: To assess whether a random sample represents a general pattern, simulate multiple panels under a random selection model to see what the simulations predict.

questions.question: Why do you need a statistic to determine if a model of random selection is supported by data?
questions.answer: You need a statistic to determine if a model of random selection is supported by data since it's impractical to evaluate thousands of bar charts individually.

questions.question: How can the distance between two numbers be measured?
questions.answer: The distance between two numbers can be measured by taking the absolute difference, denoted as |x - y|, where x and y are the numbers.

questions.question: What is the total variation distance between two distributions an extension of?
questions.answer: The total variation distance between two distributions is an extension of how distance is calculated between two numbers.

questions.question: How is the total variation distance computed when comparing proportions in each category?
questions.answer: The total variation distance is computed by first finding the difference between two proportions in each category and summing these differences.

questions.question: What does it indicate when the sum of the positive and negative differences between two sets of proportions is zero?
questions.answer: The sum of the positive and negative differences between two sets of proportions is zero, indicating that any excess in one direction is perfectly balanced by a shortfall in the other.

questions.question: How can one avoid cancellation of differences when calculating total variation distance?
questions.answer: To avoid cancellation of differences when calculating total variation distance, negative signs are dropped and the absolute values are summed.

questions.question: Why is the result divided by 2 after summing absolute values when calculating the total variation distance?
questions.answer: Once the absolute values of differences are summed, the result is divided by 2 to avoid doubling the total when calculating the total variation distance.

questions.question: How do you calculate TVD?
questions.answer: To calculate TVD, sum all the absolute differences between the distributions, which simplifies tracking positive and negative differences.

questions.question: How is TVD used in comparing the distribution of ethnicities in populations?
questions.answer: TVD is used to compare the distribution of ethnicities in populations to determine if a selection process was random.

questions.question: What do large TVD values suggest about selections being random?
questions.answer: Large TVD values suggest evidence against selections being random, indicating a need for further investigation.

questions.question: How does simulating TVD under a model help in statistical investigation?
questions.answer: Simulating TVD under a model helps understand how it varies with random samples, aiding in statistical investigation.

questions.question: Why is using a function to compute TVD beneficial?
questions.answer: Using a function to compute TVD allows for repeated calculations efficiently, especially in simulations.

questions.question: What does Total Variation Distance (TVD) measure?
questions.answer: Total Variation Distance (TVD) measures the difference between two probability distributions.

questions.question: What should the function onesimulatedtvd return?
questions.answer: Define a function, onesimulatedtvd, to return one simulated TVD value under random selection hypothesis.

questions.question: What do random selection simulations help assess?
questions.answer: Random selection simulations help assess the distribution model of eligible jurors.

questions.question: What process involves repeating an action multiple times to collect a set number of data points, such as 5000, using a for loop?
questions.answer: Using a for loop to create an array of distances involves repeating an action multiple times to collect a set number of data points, in this case, 5000.

questions.question: What does an empirical histogram visually represent in the context of simulated distances?
questions.answer: The empirical histogram visually represents the distribution of simulated distances, helping to assess the model of random selection by showing how often certain distance values occur.

questions.question: What does a total variation distance value of 0 indicate about two distributions?
questions.answer: The total variation distance is a measure of how much two distributions differ, with a value of 0 indicating identical distributions and higher values indicating greater differences.

questions.question: Why is data quality crucial in data science?
questions.answer: Data quality is crucial in data science, and involves examining how data were gathered to understand the conclusions we can draw from our analysis.

content_md:
Multiple Categories Whether jury panels are representative of the eligible population continues to be an important question. We will address it in the context of more recent panels than that of Robert Swain in the 1960s. The general process of assessment will be the same as before. But this time we will consider panelists in multiple racial and ethnic categories, not just two. From a technical perspective, this will mean that we have to come up with a new statistic to simulate. Once we have simulated it and arrived at a conclusion, we will examine some of the underlying causes of the discrepancies that we observe.  Jury Selection in Alameda County In 2010, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) of Northern California presented a  on jury selection in Alameda County, California. The report concluded that certain racial and ethnic groups are underrepresented among jury panelists in Alam

eda County, and suggested some reforms of the process by which eligible jurors are assigned to panels. In this section, we will analyze the data provided by the ACLU. As you saw in the previous section, trial jurors are selected from a larger jury panel. By law, the jury panel is supposed to be representative of the population of eligible jurors. In California, the word "representative" is clearly defined as "selected at random." Section 197 of California's Code of Civil Procedure says, "All persons selected for jury service shall be selected at random, from a source or sources inclusive of a representative cross section of the population of the area served by the court." Here "selected for jury service" means being selected to be on the jury panel.  Composition of Panels in Alameda County The focus of the study by the ACLU of Northern California was the racial and ethnic composition of 

jury panels in Alameda County. The ACLU compiled data on the composition of the jury panels in 11 felony trials in Alameda County in the years 2009 and 2010. In those panels, the total number of people who reported for jury service was 1453. The ACLU gathered demographic data on all of these prosepctive jurors, and compared those data with the composition of all eligible jurors in the county. The data are tabulated below in a table called jury. In each category, the first numerical value is the proportion of all eligible juror candidates in that category. The second value is the proportion of people in that category among those who appeared for the process of selection into the jury. The labels for the different categories are taken from the ACLU report, which says, "The category “other” includes people who identified as mixed race without identifying one primary racial identity, and ind

ividuals who did not identify a race or ethnicity." The label Asian/PI means "Asian or Pacific Islander." The label Black/AA means "Black or AfricanAmerican." Some ethnicities are overrepresented and some are underrepresented on the jury panels in the study. In particular, panelists in the Black/AA and Hispanic categories are underrepresented. A bar chart is helpful for visualizing the differences.  Comparison with Panels Selected at Random What if we select a random sample of 1,453 people from the population of eligible jurors? Will the distribution of their ethnicities look like the distribution of the panels above? We can answer these questions by using sampleproportions and augmenting the jury table with a column of the proportions in our sample. Technical note. Random samples of prospective jurors would be selected without replacement. However, when the size of a sample is small rel

ative to the size of the population, sampling without replacement resembles sampling with replacement; the proportions in the population don't change much between draws. The population of eligible jurors in Alameda County is over a million, and compared to that, a sample size of about 1500 is quite small. We will therefore sample with replacement. In the cell below, we sample at random 1453 times from the distribution of eligible jurors, and display the distribution of the random sample along with the distributions of the eligible jurors and the panel in the data. The distribution of the random sample is quite close to the distribution of the eligible population, unlike the distribution of the panels. As always, it helps to visualize. The bar chart shows that the distribution of the random sample resembles the eligible population but the distribution of the panels does not. To assess whe

ther this observation is particular to one random sample or more general, we can simulate multiple panels under the model of random selection and see what the simulations predict. But we won't be able to look at thousands of bar charts like the one above. We need a statistic that will help us assess whether or not the model or random selection is supported by the data.  A New Statistic: The Distance between Two Distributions We know how to measure how different two numbers are: if the numbers are $x$ and $y$, the distance between them is $\vert xy \vert$. Now we have to quantify the distance between two distributions. For example, we have to measure the distance between the blue and gold distributions below. For this we will compute a quantity called the total variation distance between two distributions. The calculation is as an extension of how we find the distance between two numbers.

 To compute the total variation distance, we first find the difference between the two proportions in each category. Take a look at the column Difference and notice that the sum of its entries is 0: the positive entries add up to 0.14, exactly canceling the total of the negative entries which is 0.14. This is numerical evidence of the fact that in the bar chart, the gold bars exceed the blue bars by exactly as much as the blue bars exceed the gold. The proportions in each of the two columns Panels and Eligible add up to 1, and so the giveandtake between their entries must add up to 0. To avoid the cancellation, we drop the negative signs and then add all the entries. But this gives us two times the total of the positive entries (equivalently, two times the total of the negative entries, with the sign removed). We don't need that doubling, so we divide the sum by 2. This quantity 0.14 is 

the total variation distance (TVD) between the distribution of ethnicities in the eligible juror population and the distribution in the panels. In general, the total variation distance between two distributions measures how close the distributions are. The larger the TVD, the more different the two distributions appear. Technical Note: We could have obtained the same result by just adding the positive differences. But our method of including all the absolute differences eliminates the need to keep track of which differences are positive and which are not. We will use the total variation distance between distributions as the statistic to simulate under the assumption of random selection. Large values of the distance will be evidence against random selection.  Simulating the Statistic Under the Model To see how the TVD varies across random samples, we will simulate it repeatedly under the 

model of random selection from the eligible population. Let's organize our calculation. Since we are going to be computing total variation distance repeatedly, we will first write a function that computes it for two given distributions. The function totalvariationdistance takes two arrays containing the distributions to compare, and returns the TVD between them. This function will help us calculate our statistic in each repetition of the simulation. But first let's check that it gives the right answer when we use it to compute the distance between the blue (eligible) and gold (panels) distributions above. These are the distribution in the ACLU study. This agrees with the value that we computed directly without using the function. In the cell below we use the function to compute the TVD between the distributions of the eligible jurors and one random sample. Recall that eligiblepopulation 

is the array containing the distribution of the eligible jurors, and that our sample size is 1453. In the first line, we use sampleproportions to generate a random sample from the eligible population. In the next line we use totalvariationdistance to compute the TVD between the distributions in the random sample and the eligible population. Run the cell a few times and notice that the distances are quite a bit smaller than 0.14, the distance between the distribution of the panels and the eligible jurors. We are now ready to run a simulation to assess the model of random selection.  Simulating One Value of the Statistic In the same way that we start every simulation, let's define a function onesimulatedtvd that returns one simulated value of the total variation distance under the hypothesis of random selection. The code in the body of the definition is based on the cell above.  Simulating

 Multiple Values of the Statistic Now we can apply the familiar process of using a for loop to create an array consisting of 5000 such distances.  Assessing the Model of Random Selection Here is the empirical histogram of the simulated distances. It shows that if you draw 1453 panelists at random from the pool of eligible candidates, then the distance between the distributions of the panelists and the eligible population is rarely more than about 0.05. The panels in the study, however, were not quite so similar to the eligible population. The total variation distance between the panels and the population was 0.14, shown as the red dot on the horizontal axis. It is far beyond the tail of the histogram and does not look at all like a typical distance between the distributions of a random sample and the eligible population. The simulation shows that the composition of the panels in the ACLU

 study is not consistent with the model of random selection. Our analysis supports the ACLU's conclusion that the panels were not representative of the distribution provided for the eligible jurors.  Reasons for the Bias As with most such analyses, ours doesn't say why the distributions are different. But those reasons are important to understand. If jury panels don't represent the population of eligible jurors, that can have a major effect on due process and the fairness of jury trials. The ACLU report discusses several possible reasons for the discrepancies and suggests some remedies. Some are purely practical but have a big impact. For example, while we can take for granted the high quality of random sampling in Python, the panel selection used other software. The ACLU's examination revealed that the software didn't work well, contributing to biased selection. Using better software is

 a clear remedy. More significantly, Alameda County jury panels are not selected from the entire eligible population. The  of the Superior Court of Alameda County says, "The objective of the court is to provide an accurate crosssection of the county's population. The names of jurors are selected at random from everyone who is a registered voter and/or has a driver's license or identification card issued by the Department of Motor Vehicles." This process excludes eligible jurors who are not on those lists. Having stateissued ID or being registered to vote is most likely associated with income and hence also with race and ethnicity due to historical bias against Black and Hispanic communities. This leads to underrepresentation of those groups on the panels. Those on the list have to be reached in order to inform them of their jury summons. But people in underresourced communities don't alw

ays have fixed addresses. Outofdate address records, lack of crosschecking with other lists such as those of the utilities, and so on can all contribute to biased selection. Once reached, the potential panelists still have to be able to appear. The first day of jury service is not compensated, and compensation for subsequent days is \$15 per day. While employers are required by law to excuse employees who have jury duty, they are not required to provide compensation, and some employers don't. In a region as expensive as the Bay Area, this can become a reason for panelists not to respond to jury summons. Though jurors can be excused for severe economic hardship, even the process of requesting to be excused can be out of reach for some jurors. Across the Bay, city leaders in San Francisco are attempting to reduce this form of bias by piloting a  that provides compensation of \$100 per day 

to lowincome jurors.  Data Quality We have developed a technique that helps us decide whether one distribution looks like a random sample from another. But we should examine our data sources to better understand exactly what we can conclude from our analysis. Good data science includes a thoughtful examination of how the data were gathered. Eligible Jurors. It is important to keep in mind that not everyone is eligible to serve on a jury. On its , the Superior Court of Alameda County says, "You may be called to serve if you are 18 years old, a U.S. citizen and a resident of the county or district where summoned. You must be able to understand English, and be physically and mentally capable of serving. In addition, you must not have served as any kind of juror in the past 12 months, nor have been convicted of a felony." Though the Census is a natural source of demographic information, the 

Census doesn't collect data on all these categories. Thus the ACLU had to obtain the demographics of eligible jurors in some other way. What they used was a set of estimates that had been developed by a San Diego State University professor for an Alameda County trial in 2002. Those estimates were based on the 2000 Census and also took into account the criteria required for eligibility as a juror. So we have to keep in mind that the distribution that we used for the eligible population is itself an estimate and is therefore subject to error. Moreover, as the ACLU noted, using estimates based on the 2000 Census for populations in 2010 might not be accurate due to the changing demographics in California. Thus the distribution used for the population of eligible jurors might not be accurate. Panels. It is not clear from the report exactly how the 1453 panelists were classified into the diffe

rent ethnic categories. The report says only that "attorneys ... cooperated in collecting jury pool data". Significant social, cultural, and political factors affect who gets classified or selfclassifies into each ethnic category. It is also not clear if the definitions of those categories in the panels were the same as those used in the estimates of the distribution of eligible jurors. This raises questions about the correspondence between the two distributions being compared. Moreover, the data on panelists was obtained from those who reported for service. As we have discussed, not all panelists do so. The reasons for not reporting are associated with race and ethnicity, and disproportionately affect panelists from underresourced communities. Responding to jury summons is required by law. But as the ACLU noted, at the time of the study Alameda County did not have an effective process f

or following up on prospective jurors who had been called but had failed to appear. The ACLU recommended the creation of "a failure to appear program that requires, at a minimum, the issuance of a second notice to prospective jurors." Indeed, following up on nonrespondents would reduce bias. For our analysis, we must acknowledge that we don't have the data needed to fill in the demographic information for panelists who were selected but did not report for service.  Conclusion Because of the discussion above, it is important for us to be precise about what we can conclude from our analysis.  We can conclude that the distribution provided for the panelists who reported for service does not look like a random sample from the estimated distribution in the eligible population. Our discussion, like the discussion in the ACLU report, sets out reasons for some of the differences observed between

 the two distributions and for why summoned panelists might nor report. Almost all the reasons have their roots in historical racial bias in society, and are examples of the lasting negative consequences of that bias.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does sampling with replacement mean?
choices: Sampling with replacement means dividing the population into different groups based on certain characteristics.
Sampling with replacement involves only selecting individuals once, then excluding them from further selection.
Sampling with replacement means each individual is returned to the population after being chosen, allowing them to be selected more than once.
Sampling with replacement refers to selecting a subset of individuals without returning them to the population, ensuring unique selections.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider whether individuals can be selected more than once.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does the distribution of a random sample compare to that of the population?
choices: The distribution of a random sample is always identical to that of the population regardless of the sample size.
A random sample's distribution tends to be more biased than the population distribution, regardless of sample size.
The distribution of a random sample closely matches the distribution of the eligible population when the sample size is appropriately chosen, unlike the distribution of the panels.
The distribution of a random sample never resembles that of the entire population, as samples are inherently biased.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how sample size can affect the representation of a population.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can you assess whether a random sample represents a general pattern?
choices: To assess whether a random sample represents a general pattern, simulate multiple panels under a random selection model to see what the simulations predict.
Assess the sample by comparing the average outcome against expected probability distributions.
Validate the representativeness by ensuring that the sample was unbiased at the time of collection.
You can assess it by checking if the sample size is large enough to cover all possible outcomes.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the use of simulations in evaluating patterns.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why do you need a statistic to determine if a model of random selection is supported by data?
choices: Statistics provide accurate predictions without the need for understanding the data context.
Using statistics allows you to visually inspect data trends with greater clarity compared to raw data alone.
Statistics are designed to handle data from experiments where random selection is not possible.
You need a statistic to determine if a model of random selection is supported by data since it's impractical to evaluate thousands of bar charts individually.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the impracticality of manually analyzing large volumes of data representations.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can the distance between two numbers be measured?
choices: The distance between two numbers can be measured by taking the absolute difference, denoted as |x - y|, where x and y are the numbers.
The distance between two numbers is the product of the two numbers.
The distance between two numbers can be measured by finding the average of the two numbers.
The distance between two numbers is the sum of the two numbers.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how differences are generally handled in mathematics.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the total variation distance between two distributions an extension of?
choices: The total variation distance is an extension of measuring the correlation between two variables.
The total variation distance between two distributions is an extension of how distance is calculated between two numbers.
The total variation distance is an extension of the Euclidean distance between two points.
The total variation distance is an extension of calculating the average between two distributions.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how distances are generally defined between simpler entities.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the total variation distance computed when comparing proportions in each category?
choices: The total variation distance is computed by first finding the difference between two proportions in each category and summing these differences.
The total variation distance is computed by multiplying the proportions in each category and then summing the results.
The total variation distance is computed by subtracting the smallest proportion from the largest in each category.
The total variation distance is computed by averaging the proportions and then doubling the result.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how differences between category proportions are aggregated.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does it indicate when the sum of the positive and negative differences between two sets of proportions is zero?
choices: The proportions are exactly the same in both sets.
The differences indicate a significant statistical error in measurement.
The sum of the positive and negative differences between two sets of proportions is zero, indicating that any excess in one direction is perfectly balanced by a shortfall in the other.
There is a consistent trend either higher or lower across all elements.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how balance between differences might lead to a sum of zero.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can one avoid cancellation of differences when calculating total variation distance?
choices: Use only positive differences by ignoring any negative results.
To avoid cancellation of differences when calculating total variation distance, negative signs are dropped and the absolute values are summed.
Ensure that all differences are squared before summing them.
Take the square root of each difference before adding them to avoid cancellation.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what is done with negative values in the calculation process.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the result divided by 2 after summing absolute values when calculating the total variation distance?
choices: It compensates for any potential measurement errors in the initial data set.
Dividing by 2 ensures that the distance remains normalized between 0 and 1.
Once the absolute values of differences are summed, the result is divided by 2 to avoid doubling the total when calculating the total variation distance.
To convert the summed values into percentage points for easier interpretation.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the total is calculated and why direct summation might lead to excess.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do you calculate TVD?
choices: To calculate TVD, multiply each pair of corresponding values from the distributions and sum them.
To calculate TVD, average the squared differences between the distributions.
To calculate TVD, subtract the median values of the distributions.
To calculate TVD, sum all the absolute differences between the distributions, which simplifies tracking positive and negative differences.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how differences are typically measured without cancellation of errors.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is TVD used in comparing the distribution of ethnicities in populations?
choices: TVD measures the genetic differences between individuals within a single population.
TVD calculates the average age within each ethnic group in a population.
TVD is used to compare the distribution of ethnicities in populations to determine if a selection process was random.
TVD predicts future changes in ethnic distributions based on historical data.
mcq_questions.hint: TVD helps in assessing the randomness of selection processes by comparing distributions.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do large TVD values suggest about selections being random?
choices: Large TVD values suggest evidence against selections being random, indicating a need for further investigation.
Large TVD values are irrelevant to understanding randomness in selections.
Large TVD values suggest an error in calculations but no conclusion about randomness can be drawn.
Large TVD values indicate that selections are definitely random and no further investigation is needed.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what deviations in expected outcomes might imply about the random nature of selections.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does simulating TVD under a model help in statistical investigation?
choices: Simulating TVD under a model helps increase the accuracy of measurement tools.
Simulating TVD under a model allows for prediction of future outcomes with certainty.
Simulating TVD under a model eliminates all random errors in statistical data.
Simulating TVD under a model helps understand how it varies with random samples, aiding in statistical investigation.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how simulation can reveal patterns or variations in data.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is using a function to compute TVD beneficial?
choices: Using a function to compute TVD allows for repeated calculations efficiently, especially in simulations.
Using a function to compute TVD makes the code more difficult to maintain.
A function allows for easier integration with unrelated calculations.
A function for TVD computation ensures it is only calculated once, reducing errors.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the efficiency and reusability of code in repetitive tasks.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Total Variation Distance (TVD) measure?
choices: Total Variation Distance (TVD) measures the similarity between two data points.
Total Variation Distance (TVD) measures the correlation between two random variables.
Total Variation Distance (TVD) measures the difference between two probability distributions.
Total Variation Distance (TVD) measures the variance within a single probability distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: TVD is a metric used for comparing probability distributions.

mcq_questions.prompt: What should the function onesimulatedtvd return?
choices: Define a function, onesimulatedtvd, to return one simulated TVD value under random selection hypothesis.
Define a function, onesimulatedtvd, to return a fixed TVD value for comparison.
Define a function, onesimulatedtvd, to simulate random data generation.
Define a function, onesimulatedtvd, to calculate the average of TVD values.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the purpose of simulating TVD values in statistical tests.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do random selection simulations help assess?
choices: Random selection simulations are used to analyze the time complexity of sorting operations.
Random selection simulations help assess the distribution model of eligible jurors.
Random selection simulations help understand the probability of errors in election polls.
Random selection simulations help evaluate the efficiency of selection algorithms.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the focus on jurors and fairness in selection.

mcq_questions.prompt: What process involves repeating an action multiple times to collect a set number of data points, such as 5000, using a for loop?
choices: Using a do-while loop to ensure that an action is taken at least once at the beginning of a process.
Using a while loop to execute conditional checks for infinite iterations.
Employing a recursive function to repeatedly halve a number until a base case is met.
Using a for loop to create an array of distances involves repeating an action multiple times to collect a set number of data points, in this case, 5000.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how loops can be used to accumulate data in programming.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does an empirical histogram visually represent in the context of simulated distances?
choices: The empirical histogram shows the causal relationships between different variables affecting simulated distances.
The empirical histogram visually represents the average value of simulated distances, summarizing the central tendency of data.
The empirical histogram represents the chronological order of simulated distances being generated in the simulation process.
The empirical histogram visually represents the distribution of simulated distances, helping to assess the model of random selection by showing how often certain distance values occur.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what histograms typically represent in terms of data distribution.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a total variation distance value of 0 indicate about two distributions?
choices: The distributions have slight differences but are mostly similar.
The distributions are identical, with no variation between them.
The distributions are completely different, with no overlap.
The distributions are partially overlapping but not identical.
mcq_questions.hint: A total variation distance of 0 means there is no difference at all.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is data quality crucial in data science?
choices: Data quality is crucial in data science, and involves examining how data were gathered to understand the conclusions we can draw from our analysis.
Data quality guarantees that every dataset is suitable for any type of analysis.
Data quality ensures that larger datasets always provide more accurate results.
Data quality ensures faster processing times and more efficient algorithms.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how the origin and accuracy of data impact the conclusions drawn.

title: Decisions and Uncertainty

questions.question: What are statistical tests of hypotheses used for?
questions.answer: Statistical tests of hypotheses are a framework used for decision making in various fields.

questions.question: How do statistical tests help in decision making?
questions.answer: Statistical tests help make decisions by analyzing data and hypotheses, using standard steps and terminology.

questions.question: What methodologies are involved in the general sequence of statistical tests?
questions.answer: The general sequence of statistical tests includes steps that involve both statistical and computational methodologies.

questions.question: In which fields can statistical methods in decision making be applied?
questions.answer: Statistical methods in decision making can be applied across different fields, such as biological sciences and legal system analyses.

questions.question: How do statistical and computational processes vary between disciplines?
questions.answer: Though examples may vary between disciplines, the underlying statistical and computational processes remain consistent.

questions.question: How did Mendel test the validity of his model?
questions.answer: Mendel tested his model's validity by conducting large-scale experiments, growing 929 pea plants to gather data.

questions.question: What role do statistical tests play in assessing models like Mendel's?
questions.answer: Statistical tests are used to assess the validity of models like Mendel's by comparing observed data to expected outcomes.

questions.question: What is a hypothesis in the context of data generation and hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: A hypothesis is a model or view used to explain how data are generated, and in hypothesis testing, you often choose between different views.

questions.question: What is the null hypothesis concerning data generation assumptions?
questions.answer: The null hypothesis is a specific hypothesis that assumes the data were generated at random according to certain assumptions about randomness.

questions.question: Why is the null hypothesis given its name?
questions.answer: The null hypothesis is named 'null' to emphasize that any observed differences from the hypothesis's predictions are due to random chance.

questions.question: What does the null hypothesis assume in Mendel's experiment regarding pea plant flower color?
questions.answer: In Mendel's experiment, the null hypothesis assumes that each pea plant has a 75% chance of having purple flowers, independent of other plants.

questions.question: What can you do under the null hypothesis to observe potential random samples?
questions.answer: Under the null hypothesis, you can simulate data to see what random samples might look like if the null hypothesis is true.

questions.question: What does the alternative hypothesis assert regarding the data difference from the model predictions in the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: The alternative hypothesis asserts that something other than chance explains the data difference from the model predictions in the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does the alternative hypothesis suggest about the model in question?
questions.answer: The alternative hypothesis suggests that the model in question is not accurate, but it doesn't specify why or how it fails.

questions.question: How is a test statistic used in relation to the null and alternative hypotheses?
questions.answer: A test statistic is used to choose between the null and alternative hypotheses by comparing a sample statistic to the expected distribution.

questions.question: In hypothesis testing, what role does the test statistic play regarding observed data and model predictions?
questions.answer: In hypothesis testing, the test statistic helps determine if the observed data and the model predictions have significant differences.

questions.question: How is Total Variation Distance (TVD) calculated?
questions.answer: Total Variation Distance (TVD) is calculated as half of the sum of the absolute differences between the proportions of categories in two distributions.

questions.question: What is the TVD equal to when there are only two categories?
questions.answer: When there are only two categories, the TVD is equal to the absolute difference between the proportions of one category in two distributions.

questions.question: What is the TVD in a sample distribution of [0.7, 0.3] compared to Mendel's model prediction of [0.75, 0.25]?
questions.answer: In an example where Mendel's model predicts a 'purple, white' distribution of [0.75, 0.25], a sample distribution of [0.7, 0.3] has a TVD of 0.05.

questions.question: How do you calculate the TVD for two-category distributions?
questions.answer: For two-category distributions, calculating the TVD involves evaluating how much the proportion of one category differs between the observed and the expected distribution.

questions.question: What do small values of the test statistic suggest?
questions.answer: Small values of the test statistic suggest that the sample is consistent with Mendel's model.

questions.question: What could large values of the test statistic indicate?
questions.answer: Large values of the test statistic could indicate that the model is not a good fit for the sample data.

questions.question: Why are percents preferred over proportions?
questions.answer: Percents are preferred over proportions because they are easier to interpret.

questions.question: What indicates that the alternative hypothesis is preferable to the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: A good choice of test statistic is one where either large values or small values indicate that the alternative hypothesis is preferable to the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What is the observed value of the test statistic derived from?
questions.answer: The observed value of the test statistic is the actual value calculated from the study's data, not from a simulation.

questions.question: What approach might be used if both large and small values of a statistic indicate preferring the alternative hypothesis?
questions.answer: If both large and small values of a statistic indicate preferring the alternative hypothesis, it might be better to use a distance approach to focus on just large values.

questions.question: What is the test statistic simulated based on in the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: The test statistic is simulated based on the assumptions of the model in the null hypothesis.

questions.question: How does simulating the test statistic multiple times help?
questions.answer: Simulating the test statistic multiple times provides an approximation of its probability distribution.

questions.question: How can the probability distribution of the test statistic be visualized?
questions.answer: The probability distribution of the test statistic can be visualized using a histogram.

questions.question: What does a histogram illustrate about a test statistic?
questions.answer: A histogram helps to illustrate the different possible values of a test statistic and their likelihood.

questions.question: What does the histogram visualize in relation to the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: The histogram visualizes the distribution of the test statistic as predicted by the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does the decision to reject or not reject the null hypothesis depend on?
questions.answer: The decision to reject or not reject the null hypothesis depends on the comparison between the observed test statistic and its distribution under the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What happens if the observed test statistic is not consistent with the distribution predicted by the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: If the observed test statistic is not consistent with the distribution predicted by the null hypothesis, the null hypothesis is not supported by the data.

questions.question: What do we say when the data do not support the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: When the data do not support the null hypothesis, we say that the test rejects the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does it mean if the observed test statistic is consistent with the distribution predicted by the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: If the observed test statistic is consistent with the distribution predicted by the null hypothesis, the data are said to be in line with the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does it indicate when data do not significantly deviate from a model's predictions in statistical analysis?
questions.answer: In statistical analysis, data that are consistent with a model indicate that the observed outcomes do not significantly deviate from the model's predictions.

questions.question: What challenges are involved in evaluating model predictions based on observed data?
questions.answer: The evaluation of model predictions often involves subjective interpretation of how closely observed data align with expected results, without clear universal guidelines.

questions.question: What does the consistency of an observed test statistic with its predicted distribution under the null hypothesis involve?
questions.answer: The consistency of an observed test statistic with its predicted distribution under the null hypothesis involves subjective opinion and judgment.

questions.question: What is recommended for transparency when providing test statistic values?
questions.answer: It is recommended to provide both the test statistic value and a graph of its predicted distribution under the null hypothesis for transparency.

questions.question: What should readers be allowed to do regarding the consistency of the test statistic with its predicted distribution?
questions.answer: Readers should be allowed to make their own judgments about the consistency of the test statistic with its predicted distribution.

questions.question: What does comparing observed results from different varieties necessitate?
questions.answer: Comparing observed results from different varieties necessitates assessing consistency with predictions, acknowledging potential ambiguity in interpretation.

questions.question: What are conventional cutoffs used for in statistics?
questions.answer: Conventional cutoffs are used to determine if an observed statistic is 'too far' from what is predicted by the model, using the area in the tail of a distribution.

questions.question: How can the position of an observed statistic in the tails of a distribution be interpreted?
questions.answer: The position of an observed statistic in the tails of a distribution can indicate whether it supports the alternative hypothesis.

questions.question: What does a small area in the right tail of a distribution imply about the observed statistic?
questions.answer: If the area in the right tail of the distribution is small, the statistic is considered far from values predicted by the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does the area under the curve in a histogram represent?
questions.answer: In histograms, the area under the curve represents a percentage of the distribution.

questions.question: What is the p-value referred to as?
questions.answer: The p-value is referred to as the observed significance level of the test.

questions.question: What does a p-value represent?
questions.answer: A p-value represents the probability, given a null hypothesis model, of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed.

questions.question: What does a small p-value indicate about the observed data and hypotheses?
questions.answer: A small p-value indicates that the observed data is far from what the null hypothesis predicts, suggesting that the alternative hypothesis is more supported by the data.

questions.question: What does a p-value less than 5% signify in statistical terms?
questions.answer: A p-value less than 5% is considered 'statistically significant,' meaning there is strong evidence against the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What is indicated by a p-value less than 1% in statistical significance?
questions.answer: A p-value less than 1% is considered 'highly statistically significant,' indicating even stronger evidence against the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What conclusion is typically reached when the p-value is small?
questions.answer: When the p-value is small, the conclusion is typically to reject the null hypothesis in favor of the alternative hypothesis.

questions.question: What conclusion is drawn from a statistical test with a p-value of 2.4%?
questions.answer: In a statistical test, a p-value of 2.4% is considered small, leading to the conclusion that the null hypothesis should be rejected.

questions.question: What should be included in a statistical analysis report to allow readers to make their own judgments about statistical significance?
questions.answer: When reporting statistical analysis, it is important to include the observed statistic and the p-value to allow readers to make their own judgments about statistical significance.

questions.question: What does statistical significance indicate in statistical analysis?
questions.answer: Statistical significance is a standard part of statistical analysis, indicating whether results are likely due to chance or represent a real effect.

questions.question: What should be considered when choosing a significance level like 5 percent?
questions.answer: Significance levels, like 5 percent, are conventional thresholds used to judge if a deviation is significant; however, they are subjective and should be chosen based on context.

questions.question: Why is it important to consider context when selecting a significance level in statistical testing?
questions.answer: In statistical testing, it is important to consider the context and use your judgment when selecting a significance level, rather than strictly adhering to conventional values.

questions.question: Why is it crucial to justify the selection of significance levels based on the data and circumstances?
questions.answer: Significance levels are a matter of judgment, not absolute rules, and it is crucial to justify their selection based on the data and the specific circumstances.

questions.question: Why is it important to provide the observed value of the test statistic and the p-value?
questions.answer: Always provide the observed value of the test statistic and the p-value, so that readers can independently evaluate the significance of the results.

questions.question: What is important to consider when interpreting data results?
questions.answer: Interpreting data results should be done with caution; do not disregard conventional results simply because they are unfavorable.

questions.question: What does a test result indicate if data do not support the chance model in the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: Even if data do not support the chance model in the null hypothesis, the test result does not typically explain why the model fails.

questions.question: Why should causal conclusions be made cautiously without further analysis?
questions.answer: Making causal conclusions without further analysis is discouraged unless you are conducting a randomized controlled trial.

content_md:
Decisions and Uncertainty The statistical and computational methodology that we developed for assessing models about jury selection fit into a general framework of decision making called statistical tests of hypotheses. Using statistical tests as a way of making decisions is standard in many fields and has a standard terminology. In this section we will describe the general sequence of the steps used in statistical tests, along with some terminology. Though our example is from the biological sciences, you will see that the statistical and computational steps in the process are consistent with the corresponding steps in our analyses of data from the legal system. However, the biological data are about plants, not human beings and injustice. So the context and interpretation of the calculations below are far more simple.  (18221884) was an Austrian monk who is widely recognized as the foun

der of the modern field of genetics. Mendel performed careful and largescale experiments on plants to come up with fundamental laws of genetics. Many of his experiments were on varieties of pea plants. He formulated sets of assumptions about each variety; these were his models. He then tested the validity of his models by growing the plants and gathering data. For pea plants of a particular variety, Mendel proposed the following model.  Mendel's Model  For every plant, there is a 75% chance that it will have purple flowers, and a 25% chance that the flowers will be white, regardless of the colors in all the other plants. To see whether his model was valid, Mendel grew 929 pea plants of this variety. Among these 929 plants, 705 had purple flowers. We will use these data to perform a test of hypotheses and see if Mendel's model looks good.  Step 1: The Hypotheses All statistical tests atte

mpt to choose between two views of the world. Specifically, the choice is between two views about how the data were generated. These two views are called hypotheses. The null hypothesis. This is a clearly defined model about chances. It says that the data were generated at random under clearly specified assumptions about the randomness. The word "null" reinforces the idea that if the data look different from what the null hypothesis predicts, the difference is due to nothing but chance. From a practical perspective, the null hypothesis is a hypothesis under which you can simulate data. In the example about Mendel's model for the colors of pea plants, the null hypothesis is that the assumptions of his model are good: each plant has a 75% chance of having purple flowers, independent of all other plants. Under this hypothesis, we can simulate random samples by using sampleproportions. The a

lternative hypothesis. This says that some reason other than chance made the data differ from the predictions of the model in the null hypothesis. In the example about Mendel's plants, the alternative hypothesis is simply that his model isn't good. Keep in mind that the alternative doesn't say how or why the model isn't good. It just says the model isn't good.  Step 2: The Test Statistic In order to decide between the two hypothesis, we must choose a statistic that we can use to make the decision. This is called the test statistic. We will be comparing two categorical distributions: the one in Mendel's model and the one we will get in our random sample. We want to see if these two distributions are close to each other or far apart. So a natural test statistic is the total variation distance (TVD) developed in the previous section. It turns out that with just two categories, the TVD is ra

ther simple and easy to interpret. Let's look at an example. Mendel's model says that the "purple, white" distribution is [0.75, 0.25]. Suppose the distribution in our sample came out to be [0.7, 0.3]. Because there are only two categories, something interesting happens when we calculate the TVD. First notice that $$ \vert 0.7  0.75 \vert  0.05  \vert 0.3  0.25 \vert $$ So the TVD is $$ \frac{1}{2}\Big( \vert 0.7  0.75 \vert + \vert 0.3  0.25 \vert \Big)  0.05  \vert 0.7  0.75 \vert $$ That's just the distance between the two proportions of purpleflowering plants. It is also just the distance between the two proportions of whiteflowering plants. By a bit of math that we won't do here, this is true whenever there are just two categories: the TVD is equal to the distance between the two proportions in one category. So a perfectly fine test statistic would be the distance between the sample

 proportion of purple plants and 0.75 which is the corresponding proportion in Mendel's model. Since percents are easier to interpret than proportions, we will work with percents instead. Our test statistic will be the distance between the sample percent of purple plants and 75% which is the corresponding percent in Mendel's model. $$ \Big\vert \text{sample percent of purpleflowering plants}  75 \Big\vert $$ This test statistic is a distance between the two distributions. It makes sense and is easy to use. A sample percent of around 75% will be consistent with the model, but percents much bigger or much less than 75 will make you think that the model isn't good. Therefore, small values of the distance will make you lean towards the null hypothesis. Big values of the statistic will make you lean towards the alternative. To choose a test statistic in other situations, look at the alternati

ve hypothesis. What values of the statistic will make you think that the alternative hypothesis is a better choice than the null?  If the answer is "big values," you have a good choice of statistic.  So also if the answer is "small values."  But if the answer is "both big values and small values," we recommend that you look again at your statistic. See if using a distance instead of a difference can change the answer to just "big values".  Observed Value of the Test Statistic  The observed value of the test statistic is the value of the statistic you get from the data in the study, not a simulated value. Among Mendel's 929 plants, 705 had purple flowers. The observed value of the test statistic was therefore  Step 3: The Distribution of the Test Statistic, Under the Null Hypothesis The main computational aspect of a test of hypotheses is figuring out what the model in the null hypothesis

 predicts. Specifically, we have to figure out what the values of the test statistic might be if the null hypothesis were true. The test statistic is simulated based on the assumptions of the model in the null hypothesis. That model involves chance, so the statistic comes out differently when you simulate it multiple times. By simulating the statistic repeatedly, we get a good sense of its possible values and which ones are more likely than others. In other words, we get a good approximation to the probability distribution of the statistic, as predicted by the model in the null hypothesis. As with all distributions, it is very useful to visualize this distribution by a histogram, as we have done in our previous examples. Let's go through the entire process here. We will start by assigning some known quantities to names. Next, we will define a function that returns one simulated value of 

the test statistic. Then we will use a for loop to collect 10,000 simulated values in an array. Now we can draw the histogram of these values. This is the histogram of the distribution of the test statistic predicted by the null hypothesis. Look on the horizontal axis to see the typical values of the distance, as predicted by the model. They are rather small. For example, a high proportion of the distances are in the range 0 to 1, meaning that for a high proportion of the samples, the percent of purpleflowering plants is in the range 75% $\pm$ 1%. That is, the sample percent is in the range 74% to 76%. Also note that this prediction was made using Mendel's model only, not the proportions observed by Mendel in the plants that he grew. It is time now to compare the predictions and Mendel's observation.  Step 4. The Conclusion of the Test The choice between the null and alternative hypothes

es depends on the comparison between what you computed in Steps 2 and 3: the observed value of the test statistic and its distribution as predicted by the null hypothesis. If the two are not consistent with each other, then the data do not support the null hypothesis. In other words, the alternative hypothesis is better supported by the data. We say that the test rejects the null hypothesis. If the two are consistent with each other, then the observed test statistic is in line with what the null hypothesis predicts. In other words, the null hypothesis is better supported by the data. We say that the data are consistent with the null hypothesis. In our example, the observed value of the test statistic is about 0.89, as computed in Step 2 above. Just by eye, locate roughly where 0.89 is on the horizontal axis of the histogram. You will see that it is clearly in the heart of the distributio

n predicted by Mendel's model. The cell below redraws the histogram with the observed value plotted on the horizontal axis. The observed statistic is like a typical distance predicted by the null hypothesis. The null hypothesis is Mendel's model. So our test concludes that the data are consistent with Mendel's model. Based on our data, Mendel's model looks good.  The Meaning of "Consistent" In all of our examples of assessing models there has been no doubt about whether the data were consistent with the model's predictions. They were either very far from what the model predicted, as in the examples about jury panels, or similar to what the model predicted, as in the example about Mendel's model. But outcomes are not always so clear cut. How far is "far"? Exactly what should "similar" mean? While these questions don't have universal answers, there are some guidelines and conventions that 

you can follow. But first, it is important to understand that whether the observed test statistic is consistent with its predicted distribution under the null hypothesis is a matter of subjective opinion and judgment. We recommend that you provide your judgment along with the value of the test statistic and a graph of its predicted distribution under the null. That will allow your readers to make their own judgment about whether the two are consistent. In the example above, the judgment is clear. But suppose someone grew another 929 plants of some related variety and wanted to see if Mendel's model worked for plants of that variety too. What would you conclude if their observed distance came out to be 3.2 as shown below? Is the observation based on the new variety of plants consistent with the predictions in the histogram, or not? Now the answer is not so clear. It depends on whether you

 think the red dot is too far from the bulk of the predicted values to be consistent with the prediction based on Mendel's model.  Conventional Cutoffs and the Pvalue If you don't want to use your own judgment, there are conventions that you can follow. These conventions tell us how far out into the tails is conventionally considered "too far". The conventions are based on the area in the tail, starting at the observed statistic (the red dot) and looking in the direction that makes us lean toward the alternative. In this example that's the right side, because big distances favor the alternative which says that the model isn't good. If the area of the tail is small, the observed statistic is far away from the values most commonly predicted by the null hypothesis. Remember that in a histogram, area represents percent. To find the area in the tail, we have to find the percent of distances t

hat were greater than or equal to 3.2, where the red dot is. The array distances contains the averages for all 10,000 repetitions of random sampling under Mendel's model, and differentobservedstatistic is 3.2. About 2.4% of the distances simulated under Mendel's model were 3.2 or greater. By the law of averages, we can conclude that if Mendel's model were correct for these new plants, then there is about a 2.4% chance that the test statistic would be 3.2 or more. That doesn't seem like a big chance. If Mendel's model is true for these plants, something quite unlikely has happened. This idea gives rise to the conventions.  The pValue  This chance has an impressive name. It is called the observed significance level of the test. That's a mouthful, and so it is commonly called the pvalue of the test. Definition: The pvalue of a test is the chance, based on the model in the null hypothesis, t

hat the test statistic will be equal to the observed value in the sample or even further in the direction that supports the alternative. If a pvalue is small, that means the tail beyond the observed statistic is small and so the observed statistic is far away from what the null predicts. This implies that the data support the alternative hypothesis more than they support the null. How small is "small"? According to the conventions:  If the pvalue is less than 5%, it is considered small and the result is called "statistically significant."  If the pvalue is even smaller – less than 1% – the result is called "highly statistically significant." By this convention, our pvalue of 2.4% is considered small. So the conventional conclusion would be to reject the null hypothesis and say that Mendel's model does not look good for the new plants. Formally, the result of the test is statistically sig

nificant. When you make a conclusion in this way, we recommend that you don't just say whether or not the result is statistically significant. Along with your conclusion, provide the observed statistic and the pvalue as well, so that readers can use their own judgment.  Historical Note on the Conventions The determination of statistical significance, as defined above, has become standard in statistical analyses in all fields of application. When a convention is so universally followed, it is interesting to examine how it arose. The method of statistical testing – choosing between hypotheses based on data in random samples – was developed by Sir Ronald Fisher in the early 20th century. Sir Ronald might have set the convention for statistical significance somewhat unwittingly, in the following statement in his 1925 book Statistical Methods for Research Workers. About the 5% level, he wrote

, "It is convenient to take this point as a limit in judging whether a deviation is to be considered significant or not." What was "convenient" for Sir Ronald became a cutoff that has acquired the status of a universal constant. No matter that Sir Ronald himself made the point that the value was his personal choice from among many: in an article in 1926, he wrote, "If one in twenty does not seem high enough odds, we may, if we prefer it draw the line at one in fifty (the 2 percent point), or one in a hundred (the 1 percent point). Personally, the author prefers to set a low standard of significance at the 5 percent point ..." Fisher knew that "low" is a matter of judgment and has no unique definition. We suggest that you too keep this in mind. Provide your data, make your judgment, and explain why you made it. Whether you use a conventional cutoff or your own judgment, it is important to

 keep the following points in mind.  Always provide the observed value of the test statistic and the pvalue, so that readers can decide whether or not they think the pvalue is small.  Don't look to defy convention only when the conventionally derived result is not to your liking.  Even if a test concludes that the data don't support the chance model in the null hypothesis, it typically doesn't explain why the model doesn't work. Don't make causal conclusions without further analysis, unless you are running a randomized controlled trial. We will analyze those in a later section.

title: Error Probabilities

questions.question: What do error probabilities refer to in the context of hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: Error probabilities refer to the likelihood of making an incorrect decision when testing hypotheses due to chance variation.

questions.question: What might chance variation cause when the null hypothesis is true?
questions.answer: When the null hypothesis is true, chance variation might cause the sample data to look different from what the null hypothesis predicts.

questions.question: What are the two types of errors in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: There are two types of errors in hypothesis testing: favoring the null when the alternative is true, and favoring the alternative when the null is true.

questions.question: What are the four possible outcomes in a hypothesis test?
questions.answer: In a hypothesis test, there are four possible outcomes: correct decision when null is true, error when null is favored but alternative is true, error when alternative is favored but null is true, and correct decision when alternative is true.

questions.question: How can chance variation affect conclusions in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: Chance variation can lead to incorrect conclusions in hypothesis testing despite the true state of the null or alternative hypotheses.

questions.question: What happens when a test favors the alternative hypothesis despite the null hypothesis being true?
questions.answer: A Type I error occurs when the test favors the alternative hypothesis despite the null hypothesis being true.

questions.question: What occurs when a test favors the null hypothesis despite the alternative hypothesis being true?
questions.answer: A Type II error occurs when the test favors the null hypothesis despite the alternative hypothesis being true.

questions.question: What is essential to the cutoff used for the p-value in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: The chance of a Type I error is essentially the cutoff used for the p-value in hypothesis testing.

questions.question: In hypothesis testing, what is a completely specified chance model?
questions.answer: In hypothesis testing, the null hypothesis is a completely specified chance model.

questions.question: How many times is a fair coin expected to land heads out of 2000 tosses?
questions.answer: A fair coin is expected to land heads 1000 times out of 2000 tosses.

questions.question: What do small values of the test statistic support regarding the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: Small values of the test statistic support the null hypothesis, which assumes no effect or no difference.

questions.question: How is the empirical distribution of a test statistic derived?
questions.answer: The empirical distribution of a test statistic is derived by simulating many outcomes under the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does a p-value cutoff of 5% signify in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: A p-value cutoff of 5% means that if the test statistic exceeds this cutoff, we reject the null hypothesis in favor of the alternative.

questions.question: What is the chance that the test statistic will be 45 or more with a fair coin using a 5% cutoff?
questions.answer: Even with a fair coin, there is about a 5% chance that the test statistic will be 45 or more when using a 5% cutoff.

questions.question: What is the decision rule if the test statistic is 45 or more when using a 5% cutoff?
questions.answer: The decision rule using a 5% cutoff is to conclude the coin is unfair if the test statistic is 45 or more.

questions.question: What does the cutoff for the p-value represent and what does it imply when a p% cutoff is used?
questions.answer: The cutoff for the p-value represents an error probability. If a p% cutoff is used and the null hypothesis is true, there is about a p% chance that the test will incorrectly conclude the alternative hypothesis is true.

questions.question: What is a p-value?
questions.answer: A p-value is the probability of making an error where the test favors the alternative hypothesis when the null hypothesis is actually true.

questions.question: What do p-values indicate under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true?
questions.answer: Under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true, p-values give the probability of obtaining a test result that favors the alternative hypothesis.

questions.question: Why is using a 1% p-value cutoff considered more conservative than a 5% cutoff?
questions.answer: Using a 1% p-value cutoff is more conservative than a 5% cutoff because it reduces the likelihood of concluding the alternative hypothesis when the null hypothesis is true.

questions.question: What are the four possibilities shown in the table of possible reality/test outcomes?
questions.answer: The table of possible reality/test outcomes shows four possibilities: correct result when the null is true, error when the null is true, error when the alternative is true, and correct result when the alternative is true.

questions.question: What cutoff is commonly used in randomized controlled trials of medical treatments to decide between the null hypothesis and the alternative hypothesis?
questions.answer: In randomized controlled trials of medical treatments, a cutoff of 1% is commonly used to decide between the null hypothesis and the alternative hypothesis.

questions.question: What does the null hypothesis state about the effect of the treatment?
questions.answer: The null hypothesis states that the treatment has no effect, and any differences observed are due to randomization.

questions.question: What does the alternative hypothesis suggest about the treatment?
questions.answer: The alternative hypothesis posits that the treatment has an actual effect.

questions.question: What does a 1% cutoff in hypothesis testing mean in terms of incorrectly concluding that the treatment has an effect?
questions.answer: A 1% cutoff in hypothesis testing means there's a 1% chance of incorrectly concluding that the treatment has an effect when it actually does not.

questions.question: How does random sampling help researchers in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: Random sampling allows researchers to estimate the chance of error due to chance variation in hypothesis testing.

questions.question: What might happen if 100 different research groups run separate experiments with a treatment that actually has no effect, using a 1% cutoff for the p-value?
questions.answer: If each of 100 different research groups runs a separate experiment with a treatment that actually has no effect, and uses a 1% cutoff for the p-value, by chance, one experiment might incorrectly conclude there is an effect.

questions.question: What does a p-value cutoff of 1% indicate in terms of rejecting the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: When a p-value cutoff of 1% is used, there is a 1% chance of incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis, even if there is no true effect.

questions.question: Why is replicating experiments important for confirming the validity of their results?
questions.answer: Replicating experiments helps confirm the validity of their results, as similar results should be observed in repeated trials if the original findings are accurate.

questions.question: What can lead to false positives when testing multiple hypotheses using the same data?
questions.answer: Testing multiple hypotheses using the same data can lead to false positives due to chance, especially when the hypotheses involve treatments with no real effect.

questions.question: Why is it crucial for other researchers to be able to replicate an experiment?
questions.answer: It is crucial for other researchers to be able to replicate an experiment to ensure its findings are not due to random chance.

questions.question: Why is it important to consider how many different effects were tested in a study before a significant result was reported?
questions.answer: When reading a study, it's important to consider how many different effects were tested before a significant result was reported, as multiple tests can lead to spurious findings.

questions.question: How can replicating an experiment help validate its conclusions?
questions.answer: Replicating an experiment and obtaining a significant result for the same effect can help validate the conclusions of the original study, minimizing the risk of false findings due to data snooping.

questions.question: When should a statistically significant result from an experiment be used with caution?
questions.answer: A statistically significant result from an experiment should be used with caution if it was derived after conducting numerous tests without prior justification.

questions.question: What often happens when efforts are made to reduce one type of error in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: Efforts to reduce one type of error in hypothesis testing often lead to an increase in the other type of error.

questions.question: Why is balancing errors in hypothesis testing considered challenging?
questions.answer: In hypothesis testing, balancing errors is challenging because decreasing false positives typically increases false negatives, and vice versa.

questions.question: What are the two types of errors discussed in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: False positives and false negatives are two types of errors discussed in hypothesis testing.

questions.question: What is a consequence of adjusting a test to avoid one kind of mistake in hypothesis testing?
questions.answer: Adjusting a test to avoid one kind of mistake often amplifies the likelihood of the opposite mistake in hypothesis testing.

content_md: Error Probabilities In the process by which we decide which of two hypotheses is better supported by our data, the final step involves a judgment about the consistency of the data and the null hypothesis. While this step results in a good decision a vast majority of the time, it can sometimes lead us astray. The reason is chance variation. For example, even when the null hypothesis is true, chance variation might cause the sample to look quite different from what the null hypothesis predicts. Wrong Conclusions If you are testing a null hypothesis against the alternative that the null hypothesis isn't true, then there are four ways of classifying reality and the result of the test. | | Test Favors the Null | Test Favors the Alternative | |:|:|:| |Null is True| Correct result | Error | |Alternative is True| Error | Correct result | In two out of the four cells of this table, the test resu lt is wrong. One type of error occurs if the test favors the alternative hypothesis when in fact the null hypothesis is true. The other type of error occurs if the test favors the null hypothesis when in fact the alternative hypothesis is true. Since the null hypothesis is a completely specified chance model, the first type of error has a chance that we can estimate. The answer turns out to be essentially the cutoff that we use for the pvalue. Let's see how. The Chance of an Error Suppose you want to test whether a coin is fair or not. Then the hypotheses are: Null: The coin is fair. That is, the results are like draws made at random with replacement from Heads, Tails. Alternative: The coin is not fair. Suppose you are going to test this hypothesis based on 2000 tosses of the coin. You would expect a fair coin to land heads 1000 times out of 2000, so a reasonable test statistic to use i s $$ \text{test statistic} \Big\vert \text{number of heads} 1000 \Big\vert $$ Small values of this statistic favor the null hypothesis, and large values favor the alternative. We have simulated this statistic under the null hypothesis many times, and drawn its empirical distribution. The area to the right of 45, colored gold, is just under 5%. Large values of the test statistic favor the alternative. So if you wanted to use a 5% cutoff for the pvalue, your decision rule would be to conclude that the coin is unfair if the test statistic comes out to be 45 or more. However, as the figure shows, a fair coin can produce test statistics with values 45 or more. In fact it does so with chance approximately 5%. Summary: If the coin is fair and our test uses a 5% cutoff for deciding whether it is fair or not, then there is about a 5% chance that the test will wrongly conclude that the coin is unfair. The Cutoff for the pvalue is an Error Probability The example above is a special case of a general fact: If you use a $p$% cutoff for the pvalue, and the null hypothesis happens to be true, then there is about a $p$% chance that your test will conclude that the alternative is true. Here is the table of the four possible reality/test outcomes again. Probabilities in the top row are computed under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. The pvalue is the probability of the error shown in red. | | Test Favors the Null | Test Favors the Alternative | |:|:|:| |Null is True| Correct result |

title: Comparing Two Samples

questions.question: What does comparing two samples involve?
